books.favoriteQuote: The quality of our engagement with the world: Kundera encourages us to reclaim the pleasure of slow experiences as a counter to the increasingly rapid pace of modern life.
books.recommendedFor: Those who appreciate philosophical fiction and cultural criticism

books.title: Pulling Your Own Strings
books.author: Wayne W. Dyer
books.summary: A compelling guide on asserting personal independence and power, encouraging us to transcend a victim mentality and adopt a more proactive stance in controlling our lives.
keyTakeaways: Victim mentality is often a learned behavior that can be unlearned
Challenge labels that do not resonate with your identity
Your life is the sum of your choices
Manage your emotional reactions and refuse to let others manipulate your feelings or decisions
Foster self-respect by teaching others how you wish to be treated
books.favoriteQuote: Your life is the sum of your choices.
books.recommendedFor: Anyone feeling trapped by external circumstances or internal conflicts

books.title: El Poder de Estar Solo
books.author: Brian Alba
books.summary: Dives into blood various philosophical ideas about solitude into a single narrative through self-discovery despite feeling the compulsive somewhere lonely.
keyTakeaways: Self-companionship is crucial: Being content alone is essential
Viewing perspective as allies rather than enemies can transform experiences
Recognizing and using difficult language to foster a healthy societal vision
Personal desires are seen as steps towards success and gratitude
books.favoriteQuote: Acknowledging mistakes in learning opportunities and choosing self-blame.
books.recommendedFor: Those seeking to find strength and peace in solitude

books.title: Permanent Record
books.author: Edward Snowden
books.summary: An autobiographical account of a former intelligence contractor who became one of the most famous whistleblowers in history.
keyTakeaways: The power of information and surveillance: The American intelligence community collects vast amounts of data from citizens
There are Moral responsibilities that transcend professional obligations
Privacy vs security: The need for national security and the right to personal privacy
Impact of technology on human rights: Digital surveillance can undermine individual freedoms
Ethical considerations in intelligence: Ethics of government operations and the limits of lawful surveillance
books.favoriteQuote: Privacy is security. The need for national security and the right to personal privacy.
books.recommendedFor: Those concerned about digital privacy and surveillance

books.title: Elon Musk
books.author: Ashlee Vance
books.summary: Chronicles the extraordinary life of one of the most intriguing entrepreneurs of our time, providing a detailed look into Musk's relentless drive and visionary ambitions.
keyTakeaways: Innovation is driven by relentless pursuit and a clear vision for the future
Working towards a bigger "smarter" design attracts experts in ways money could never
Resilience: Be a little delusional and very persistent
Think in systems: Move fast. Optimize but not too early
books.favoriteQuote: Innovation is driven by relentless pursuit and a clear vision for the future.
books.recommendedFor: Entrepreneurs and those interested in innovation and space exploration

books.title: Quantum Entanglement
books.author: Jed Brody
books.summary: An exploration into one of the most fascinating aspects of quantum physics, presenting complex topics in an accessible manner.
keyTakeaways: Quantum entanglement defies classical physics: Particles can instantaneously affect each other across vast distances
The experimental approaches to understanding entanglement
Quantum entanglement has profound implications for the future of technology
The paradoxical nature of quantum reality that challenges traditional notions of space and time
books.favoriteQuote: Quantum entanglement has profound implications for the future of technology, including quantum computing and secure communications.
books.recommendedFor: Those interested in quantum physics and cutting-edge science

books.title: Shoe Dog
books.author: Phil Knight
books.summary: Details the founding and building of Nike, one of the world's most iconic and innovative athletic brands.
keyTakeaways: Embrace risks and the unknown: Step into uncharted territory to find success
The power of perseverance: Knight emphasizes how his persistence through numerous challenges was crucial
Build a strong, cohesive team: Known as the "Crazy Gang"
Maintain integrity and authenticity: It helped steer Nike through many potential pitfalls
Value the journey: The process of building Nike, with all its highs and lows, was as rewarding as the outcomes
books.favoriteQuote: The power of perseverance. Throughout the narrative, Knight emphasizes how his persistence through numerous challenges was crucial to Nike's eventual success.
books.recommendedFor: Entrepreneurs and anyone interested in business success stories

books.title: Unreasonable Hospitality
books.author: Will Guidara
books.summary: Explores the transformative power of going above and beyond in the hospitality industry, sharing how exceptional service can create unforgettable experiences.
keyTakeaways: Go beyond what is expected: Always strive to surprise and delight guests by providing more than they anticipate
Foster a culture of collaboration: Success is driven by a team that shares a vision of exceptional service
Embrace technology to enhance service
Commit to excellence: Every detail counts in crafting an outstanding guest experience
Apply hospitality principles universally: The 95-5% rule
books.favoriteQuote: It's not just about running a successful restaurant; it's about setting a new standard in any service-oriented field.
books.recommendedFor: Business leaders, service professionals, and hospitality entrepreneurs

books.title: El camino de YC
books.author: Tiago Sada
books.summary: A Spanish-language guide to Y Combinator by Tiago Sada and fellow YC alumni. They share firsthand experiences navigating the world's top startup accelerator — from the application process to Demo Day — offering practical advice for founders considering the program.
books.recommendedFor: Founders considering applying to Y Combinator or anyone interested in the startup accelerator ecosystem
keyTakeaways: YC looks for founders with deep conviction and a clear understanding of their users, not just polished pitches.
The accelerator's real value lies in the network, mentorship, and intensity of focus during the batch — not just the funding.
The application process itself is a useful exercise in clarifying your startup's vision and value proposition.
Speed of execution and willingness to iterate quickly are the traits YC rewards most.
books.personalNotes: A must-read for any Latin American founder considering YC. Tiago and the other alumni break down what the experience is actually like from the inside, cutting through the mystique. Practical, honest, and motivating.

books.title: Never Enough
books.author: Andrew Wilkinson
books.summary: The founder of MetaLab shares his journey of building and selling companies, exploring the psychology of wealth and the endless pursuit of 'more' in entrepreneurship.
books.recommendedFor: Entrepreneurs and business leaders
keyTakeaways: The pursuit of 'more' is a psychological trap — no amount of money will ever feel like enough if your sense of worth is tied to your net worth.
Building companies can become an addiction. Recognizing the compulsive patterns behind entrepreneurship is the first step to a healthier relationship with work.
Comparison is the enemy of satisfaction. The goalposts always move when you measure yourself against others.
True wealth is having the freedom to do what matters to you — not the accumulation of more for its own sake.
books.personalNotes: Wilkinson is refreshingly honest about the dark side of entrepreneurial success. Most business books glorify the hustle; this one asks whether the hustle is worth it. A necessary counterbalance for anyone chasing the next milestone.

books.title: Nada nunca termina
books.author: Alberto Villarreal
books.summary: Alberto Villarreal's first non-fiction work, written alongside his psychoanalysis process. He explores heartbreak, the end of love, and the grieving process — sharing his own romantic disappointments alongside reflections and reading recommendations to help readers accept that love can exist even after a relationship ends.
books.recommendedFor: Readers going through heartbreak or processing the end of a relationship
keyTakeaways: Letting go of someone does not mean erasing them from your story — endings are part of the narrative, not its negation.
Grief after a breakup is legitimate and deserves the same respect and processing as any other form of loss.
Vulnerability is not weakness — writing and reflecting openly about pain is a form of strength and healing.
Moving on is not a single event but an ongoing process that unfolds at its own pace.
books.personalNotes: Villarreal writes with the kind of vulnerability that makes you feel less alone in your own pain. This book doesn't offer quick fixes — it sits with you in the mess of heartbreak and reminds you that endings are not failures. A healing read.

books.title: Todo lo que no me deja olvidarte
books.author: Alberto Villarreal
books.coverImage: book-covers/2025/4 todo lo que no me deja olvidarte.webp
books.summary: A novel that dives into love, grief, friendship, and personal growth. The story follows Edgar, whose emotional scars from the tragic death of his ex-boyfriend Jorge continue to haunt him even after he moves to Mexico City. It explores how we carry loss and learn to live alongside it.
books.recommendedFor: Readers of contemporary Mexican fiction and stories exploring grief and young adulthood
keyTakeaways: Grief does not follow a timeline — the people we lose stay with us in unexpected ways.
Moving to a new place cannot outrun emotional baggage; healing requires confronting pain directly.
Friendship and human connection are essential anchors during the process of recovering from loss.
Identity and love are intertwined — losing someone can feel like losing part of yourself.
books.personalNotes: Villarreal captures the weight of carrying someone you've lost into every new chapter of your life. The Mexico City setting adds a layer of restless searching that mirrors Edgar's internal journey. Honest and deeply felt.

books.title: Tu pareja ideal
books.author: Logan Ury
books.summary: Behavioral scientist and dating coach Logan Ury draws on psychological research to identify the mental barriers that sabotage our search for lasting love. The book offers practical, science-backed strategies for overcoming dating blind spots and building meaningful romantic relationships.
books.recommendedFor: Anyone navigating the dating world who wants a practical, evidence-based approach to relationships
keyTakeaways: Great relationships are built through intentional decisions, not found by chance — dating is a skill that can be improved.
Our brains have predictable biases (the Romanticizer, the Maximizer, the Hesitater) that sabotage our dating choices.
Stop looking for a 'spark' on the first date; instead, focus on whether the person would make a good long-term partner.
Taking action and making real behavioral changes matters more than simply understanding your patterns.
books.favoriteQuote: Great relationships are built, not found.
books.personalNotes: One of the most practical dating books I've come across. Ury's framework of the three dating tendencies — Romanticizer, Maximizer, Hesitater — is eye-opening. It's not about finding 'the one,' it's about becoming someone who can build something lasting.

books.title: The Ride of a Lifetime
books.author: Robert Iger
books.coverImage: book-covers/2025/6 The ride of a lifetime.webp
books.summary: The CEO of Disney shares lessons from his remarkable career, leading one of the world's most beloved companies through times of profound change and transformation.
books.recommendedFor: Business leaders and entertainment industry enthusiasts
keyTakeaways: The relentless pursuit of perfection is not the goal — the pursuit of genuine innovation through calculated risk-taking is what creates breakthroughs.
Leading through times of change requires three things: optimism, courage, and an openness to what's next.
Iger's acquisition strategy (Pixar, Marvel, Lucasfilm, Fox) shows that bold bets backed by deep understanding of brand value can transform a company.
Treat every person you work with with fairness and respect. Your reputation as a leader is built on how you handle the small interactions, not just the big deals.
books.personalNotes: Iger's story is a masterclass in leadership through transformation. His ability to identify what Disney needed — creative revitalization through strategic acquisitions — and then execute with patience and conviction is remarkable. The Pixar acquisition alone is worth the read.

books.title: Letting You Go
books.author: Billie Brooks
books.summary: A short, reflective poetry and prose collection about loss, grief, heartbreak, and the courage it takes to turn the page. Brooks writes with vulnerability and resilience, offering words of comfort for readers going through painful transitions.
books.recommendedFor: Readers going through loss or heartbreak who are looking for a short, contemplative read
keyTakeaways: Turning the page — even when it terrifies you — is often the best decision you will ever make.
Grief, heartbreak, and loss are not obstacles to avoid but experiences that shape and deepen who you are.
Resilience is not about moving on quickly; it is about sitting with the pain and still choosing to move forward.
books.favoriteQuote: There will come a day when you realize turning the page was the best decision you have ever made. There is so much more to the book than the page you were stuck on.
books.personalNotes: Short but powerful. Brooks doesn't try to fix your pain — she acknowledges it and reminds you that there's more ahead. The kind of book you keep on your nightstand during hard seasons.

books.title: Social Engineering
books.author: Christopher Hadnagy
books.summary: The science of human hacking - understanding how social engineers exploit human psychology to breach security systems and how to defend against these attacks.
books.recommendedFor: Security professionals and anyone interested in cybersecurity
keyTakeaways: Social engineering attacks exploit human psychology — trust, authority, fear, and helpfulness — rather than technical vulnerabilities.
The most dangerous hackers don't break into systems; they convince people to open the door for them.
Understanding body language, micro-expressions, and communication patterns is as important to security as firewalls and encryption.
Everyone is vulnerable to social engineering. Awareness and training are the only reliable defenses.
books.personalNotes: This book changes how you see every interaction. Once you understand how social engineers think, you start recognizing manipulation patterns everywhere — in sales, marketing, politics, and daily life. Surprisingly relevant beyond just cybersecurity.

books.title: El laberinto de la felicidad
books.author: Alex Rovira & Francesc Miralles
books.coverImage: book-covers/2025/9 El laberinto de la felicidad.webp
books.summary: A short, inspiring fable about Ariadna, a woman who has lost her job and the meaning in her life, and enters a fantastical 'labyrinth of happiness.' Through encounters with various characters, she discovers that knowing and accepting yourself is the prerequisite to finding true happiness.
books.recommendedFor: Readers looking for a quick, accessible read on happiness and self-discovery
keyTakeaways: Happiness is not a destination but the way you walk through life.
We see happiness in what is far away, but it is actually much closer than we imagine.
Self-knowledge and self-acceptance are the necessary steps before you can truly be happy.
There are three opinions that shape our reality: the opinion we have of others, the opinion we believe others have of us, and the opinion we have of ourselves.
books.favoriteQuote: La felicidad no es un destino, sino la forma en la que caminas por la vida.
books.personalNotes: A quick, fable-style read that packs a surprising amount of wisdom. The labyrinth metaphor works beautifully — happiness isn't about finding the exit, it's about how you navigate the journey. The insight about the three opinions that shape reality stayed with me.

books.title: Historia de México
books.author: Captivating History
books.summary: A comprehensive yet accessible overview of Mexican history from pre-Hispanic civilizations and the Spanish conquest through independence, the Revolution, and into the modern era. It covers the key events, figures, and movements that shaped Mexico as a nation.
books.recommendedFor: Readers looking for a broad, introductory overview of Mexican history
keyTakeaways: Mexico's identity was forged through the collision and fusion of indigenous civilizations and Spanish colonization — a duality that persists today.
The Mexican Revolution was not a single event but a complex series of movements driven by vastly different leaders and ideologies.
Understanding the pre-Hispanic past — Olmec, Maya, Aztec — is essential to understanding modern Mexican culture and identity.
Mexico's modern challenges are deeply rooted in historical patterns of centralized power, foreign intervention, and social inequality.
books.personalNotes: As a Mexican, understanding our own history is fundamental. This book gives a broad overview that connects the dots from the ancient civilizations to where we are today. It helps you see how deeply the past shapes the present.

books.title: The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue
books.author: V.E. Schwab
books.coverImage: book-covers/2025/11 The invisible life of addie larue.webp
books.summary: A fantasy novel about a woman cursed to be forgotten by everyone she meets, spanning centuries of solitude until she meets someone who remembers her.
books.recommendedFor: Fantasy and romance readers
keyTakeaways: The curse of being forgotten by everyone you meet is a powerful metaphor for invisibility — the feeling of going through life without being truly seen.
Addie's story explores the tension between freedom and connection: she has infinite time but cannot form lasting bonds.
Memory and identity are inseparable — without someone to remember you, who are you really?
The novel asks what makes a life meaningful: is it the marks we leave on others, or the experiences we have ourselves?
books.personalNotes: Schwab created something beautiful here. The idea of living forever but being forgotten by everyone is haunting. It makes you think about what you'd sacrifice for time and what you'd sacrifice for love. The centuries-spanning narrative keeps you hooked.

books.title: The Anxious Generation
books.author: Jonathan Haidt
books.summary: How the great rewiring of childhood is causing an epidemic of mental illness, exploring the impact of smartphones and social media on Gen Z's mental health.
books.recommendedFor: Parents, educators, and anyone interested in digital culture's impact
keyTakeaways: The shift from a play-based childhood to a phone-based childhood is the primary driver of the youth mental health crisis that began around 2012.
Social media exploits the developing adolescent brain — the need for social validation becomes an addictive loop.
The four foundational harms: social deprivation, sleep deprivation, attention fragmentation, and addiction.
Solutions require collective action: delaying smartphone access, phone-free schools, more unsupervised play, and age-appropriate internet access.
books.personalNotes: One of the most important books of our time. Haidt presents overwhelming evidence that we've conducted a massive uncontrolled experiment on an entire generation. The solutions he proposes are practical and urgent. Everyone — not just parents — should read this.

books.title: Anxious Attachment Recovery
books.author: Linda Hill
books.summary: A self-help guide that explores the root causes and effects of anxious attachment style, rooted in inconsistent childhood caregiving. Hill provides practical strategies to move from insecure relationship patterns to confident, secure attachment through self-awareness, boundary-setting, and intentional behavioral change.
books.recommendedFor: Anyone who recognizes anxious patterns in their relationships and wants practical tools to develop more secure attachment
keyTakeaways: Anxious attachment originates from inconsistent caregiving and manifests as a deep fear of abandonment and constant need for reassurance.
Acknowledging your attachment style is the crucial first step — you cannot change what you do not recognize.
Practical tools like visualization exercises, affirmations, and healthy boundary-setting can rewire anxious patterns over time.
A secure attachment style — marked by trust, comfort, and a balance of closeness and autonomy — is achievable with consistent practice.
books.personalNotes: Understanding attachment theory is one of those things that changes how you see every relationship in your life. Hill makes the concepts accessible and actionable. The exercises are practical and the progress feels real when you commit to the work.

books.title: La Sombra del Viento
books.author: Carlos Ruiz Zafón
books.summary: A mystery novel set in post-war Barcelona about a young boy who discovers a mysterious book that will change his life, leading him into a labyrinth of secrets.
books.recommendedFor: Mystery lovers and Spanish literature enthusiasts
keyTakeaways: Books have a life of their own — they find the right reader at the right time, and the relationship between reader and book is as real as any human connection.
Barcelona itself becomes a character in the novel — its streets, shadows, and secrets mirror the mysteries of the plot.
The Cemetery of Forgotten Books is one of the most beautiful metaphors in literature: a place where abandoned stories wait for someone to give them life again.
Zafón masterfully weaves love, betrayal, and obsession into a story that reminds you why you fell in love with reading in the first place.
books.personalNotes: One of the most captivating novels I've ever read. Zafón's Barcelona pulls you in like a labyrinth you don't want to escape. The writing is gorgeous, the mystery is gripping, and the love letter to books and reading is something every reader will feel in their soul.

books.title: Free Will
books.author: Sam Harris
books.summary: A philosophical exploration arguing that free will is an illusion, examining the implications for morality, law, and personal responsibility.
books.recommendedFor: Philosophy enthusiasts and those interested in consciousness
keyTakeaways: Free will is an illusion — our thoughts and actions arise from prior causes (genetics, upbringing, brain chemistry) that we did not choose.
Even the feeling of choosing is itself a neurological event that happens before we become conscious of it.
Accepting the illusion of free will doesn't undermine morality — it actually fosters greater compassion and less judgment toward others.
If people don't truly author their own actions, our approach to criminal justice and blame needs fundamental rethinking.
books.personalNotes: Harris makes a provocative argument in a very short book. Whether you agree or not, it forces you to examine assumptions you've never questioned. The implications for how we judge others — and ourselves — are profound. A quick read that stays with you.

books.title: The Next Everything
books.summary: A forward-looking exploration of the technologies and ideas poised to reshape every aspect of how we live, work, and connect. The book examines the converging forces of innovation that are redefining industries, relationships, and human potential.
books.recommendedFor: Technology enthusiasts and anyone curious about the future of innovation
keyTakeaways: The pace of technological change is accelerating — what took decades now takes years, and what took years now takes months.
The convergence of multiple technologies (AI, biotech, energy, connectivity) creates possibilities that no single innovation could achieve alone.
Adapting to the next wave of change requires not just technical skills but new ways of thinking about work, identity, and purpose.
The future belongs to those who can embrace uncertainty and learn faster than the rate of change.
books.personalNotes: A thought-provoking look at what's coming next. The book connects dots across industries and technologies in a way that makes you see the bigger picture. Good for expanding your perspective on where the world is heading.

books.title: Once a Bitcoin Miner
books.author: Ethan Lou
books.summary: A firsthand account of the cryptocurrency gold rush, exploring the world of Bitcoin mining and the characters drawn to this digital frontier.
books.recommendedFor: Cryptocurrency enthusiasts and tech readers
keyTakeaways: The Bitcoin mining world attracts a colorful cast of characters — dreamers, criminals, idealists, and opportunists — all drawn to the promise of digital gold.
Mining is not just a technical process; it's a window into human greed, ambition, and the eternal search for a shortcut to wealth.
The environmental cost of Bitcoin mining is real and growing, creating a tension between innovation and sustainability.
Lou's personal journey through the crypto world shows how easy it is to get swept up in a movement and how hard it is to maintain perspective.
books.personalNotes: Lou writes about crypto not as a technical manual but as a human story. The characters he encounters are fascinating, and his own journey from curiosity to deep involvement mirrors what so many experienced during the crypto boom. Entertaining and insightful.

books.title: Invent and Wander
books.author: Jeff Bezos
books.summary: A collection of Jeff Bezos's writings and annual letters to shareholders, revealing the principles and long-term thinking that built Amazon.
books.recommendedFor: Entrepreneurs and business strategists
keyTakeaways: Long-term thinking is the ultimate competitive advantage. Bezos consistently chose to sacrifice short-term profits for long-term market position.
Customer obsession — not competitor obsession — is the foundation of Amazon's strategy. Start with the customer and work backward.
Day 1 mentality: treat every day like it's the first day of the company. Day 2 is stasis, followed by irrelevance, followed by death.
High-velocity decision making matters more than perfect decision making. Most decisions are reversible — make them fast with about 70% of the information you wish you had.
books.personalNotes: Reading Bezos's shareholder letters in sequence is like watching a masterclass in long-term strategic thinking unfold in real time. The consistency of his principles over decades is remarkable. The Day 1 philosophy and the emphasis on high-velocity decisions are frameworks I keep coming back to.

books.title: Trillion Dollar Coach
books.author: Eric Schmidt, Jonathan Rosenberg, Alan Eagle
books.summary: The leadership playbook of Bill Campbell, Silicon Valley's most legendary coach who mentored Steve Jobs, Eric Schmidt, and other tech titans.
books.recommendedFor: Leaders, managers, and tech industry professionals
keyTakeaways: Bill Campbell's secret was simple: he led with love. He genuinely cared about the people he coached, and that trust became the foundation for extraordinary performance.
Great coaching is about making other people better. Campbell always asked 'How can I help?' before anything else.
Build community first, then tackle problems. Campbell believed that when teams trust each other, they can solve anything.
Courage is the most important quality in a leader. Campbell pushed people to make the hard decisions they were avoiding.
books.personalNotes: A beautiful tribute to a man who shaped Silicon Valley from behind the scenes. Campbell's approach — leading with genuine care for people while demanding excellence — is the kind of leadership the world needs more of. The anecdotes from Jobs, Schmidt, and others are priceless.

books.title: If Cats Disappeared from the World
books.author: Genki Kawamura
books.coverImage: book-covers/2025/20 If cats disappeared from the world.webp
books.summary: A philosophical tale about a man who learns he has only days to live and makes a deal with the devil to extend his life by removing things from the world.
books.recommendedFor: Readers who enjoy philosophical fiction
keyTakeaways: We don't appreciate the things in our lives until we imagine losing them. The thought experiment of removing one thing at a time reveals what truly matters.
The devil's bargain — trading something from the world for one more day of life — forces impossible choices about value and meaning.
Connection, memory, and the small daily experiences are more valuable than we realize in the rush of everyday life.
Kawamura asks the ultimate question: what would you be willing to lose to keep living, and at what point does life without those things stop being worth it?
books.personalNotes: A deceptively simple story that hits you harder than you expect. The premise — what if you could trade things from the world for more time alive — sounds like a thought experiment, but Kawamura turns it into something deeply emotional. It made me think about what I take for granted every day.

books.title: El Viejo y el Mar
books.author: Ernest Hemingway
books.summary: In this classic novel we see the journey of the 'old man' fishing the biggest fish the town has ever seen. In general we see the struggle of being alone against challenges and the obstacles despite the experience. We see resilience, and passion through tough times.
books.recommendedFor: Those who appreciate classic literature and tales of perseverance
keyTakeaways: Heroism is not about triumph or material success, but about personal struggle, integrity, and the will to keep striving
Take pride in personal abilities and achievements. Getting old and having more experience is a source of pride, not shame
Recognize the deep connection between all living beings. Everything is interconnected
Even in the face of overwhelming odds, one must not give up. The fisherman faces relentless struggle and yet shows the human spirit's capacity to endure hardship
books.favoriteQuote: This book is narrated in great detail. It catches you and captivates you in such a way that it feels like you are accompanying the fisherman.
books.personalNotes: It is a very enjoyable read, I definitely recommend it.

books.title: El Arte de Amar
books.author: Erich Fromm
books.summary: In this book, the author takes a deep dive into the concept of love. We explore the different kinds of understanding love as an art, and to the sociological context of the world, as well as the spiritual implications of loving. It is highly theoretical, yet very down to earth.
books.recommendedFor: Anyone seeking to understand and improve the quality of their relationships
keyTakeaways: It is an art, the fruit of learning. We have to learn to love
Falling in love and staying in love are not the same. Love is active concern. Love only begins to develop when we love someone we don't need
The basis of selfishness is the lack of self-love. They don't know how to love, including themselves
Parents' love of life is as contagious as their anxiety. Inherited perspectives

Love is giving. Giving constitutes the highest expression of potential, in the very act of giving I experience my strength, my wealth, my power. Giving produces more joy than receiving, not because it is a deprivation but because in the act of giving is the expression of my vitality
books.favoriteQuote: Love is giving. Giving constitutes the highest expression of potential.
books.personalNotes: In general I think we all need to read this book. Even though I don't agree with its binary perspective on love, I do think there's a lot to learn and reflect from the ideas discussed in this book. It is not necessarily an easy read, but if you want to improve the quality of your relationships, I would absolutely recommend this book.

books.title: Quantum Supremacy
books.author: Michio Kaku
books.summary: In this book the author explores the history, the present and the potential future of quantum computing. It is crazy to think the amount of doors and possibilities this technology enables. Cybersecurity, biology and health, energetic autonomy these are only examples of industries it can potentially radically transform. We are about to live one of the biggest transformations we have yet seen as a species.
books.recommendedFor: Technology enthusiasts and those curious about the future of computing
keyTakeaways: There are different approaches to creating quantum computers, using lasers, using low temperatures, crystals, etc. It is all about leveraging quantum states to run multiple operations at the same time
In theory quantum computers are infinitely faster than regular computers due to superposition
There are still problems to be solved given the probabilistic nature of the technology, but we are very close to doing unimaginable things
What if we could simulate complex molecules in minutes. What if we could mix technologies like AI and quantum computers to regulate nuclear fusion?
What are the implications of quantum physics and quantum computers on spirituality and religion? What does it mean that the quantum waves only collapse when measured?
books.favoriteQuote: We are about to live one of the biggest transformations we have yet seen as a species.
books.personalNotes: In general if you like technology and want to see what the future holds from the mind of a leading expert, I would absolutely recommend this book. It was well written, backed by science, and very up to date. It gives enough context for you to understand the main ideas.

books.title: Nonviolence
books.author: Mark Kurlansky
books.summary: In this book the author explores the history of non-violence. The active pursuit of peace through the resistance to the use of violence as a mean. It deals with topics such as politics, religion, power and rebellion. It focuses on the things we might learn from the resolution of previous conflicts.
books.recommendedFor: Those interested in political philosophy and conflict resolution
keyTakeaways: A saint is less dangerous than a rebel. Stories of the death can be reinterpreted and retold
War is a constant act of reciprocal action. There are no innocents if involved in violence
The practice of violence changes the world, but the most probable change is a more violent world - Hannah Arendt

The justification of war or violence is as old as us. The argument that people deserved to be slaved was something Aristotle wrote about, Jefferson said all men were made equal, but blacks were less equal, ect. It is the oldest fallacy

Is the source of violence not human nature, but the lack of imagination? Do you have to be a perfect human being to refuse war? To what extent not doing everything right disqualifies progress or improvement?
books.favoriteQuote: The practice of violence changes the world, but the most probable change is a more violent world.
books.personalNotes: In general I would absolutely recommend this book. Specially if you want to obtain a different perspective on contemporary conflicts. It explores the use of violence as a tool for political control. I found particularly interesting the relationship between religion and war.

books.title: La ridícula idea de no volver a verte
books.author: Rosa Montero
books.summary: In this book the author uses the story of Marie Curie as a way to talk about her own life. It deals with topics such as women in a men dominated world, the lost of a loved one, family, professional success and pain.
books.recommendedFor: Readers interested in biographical fiction and women's stories
keyTakeaways: The true pain is unspeakable. The first thing that pain tears from you are words
To live we have to narrate ourselves. It gives meaning to chaos. Now I know that I write to try to give evil and pain a meaning that I know they do not have
The fear of the masculine world of femininity, of women. What is the place of women in this world?
Excess of idealization for aesthetics. There are more valuable things that get overlooked
For women, the expectativas for the personal life and the professional achievements tend to get mixed up. Having a double standard vs men
books.favoriteQuote: To live we have to narrate ourselves. It gives meaning to chaos.
books.personalNotes: In general it is a great piece of literature. It is well written, relatable, and personal. I like the historical parallel it creates to talk about the things that have changed, and the things that haven't. I would absolutely recommend this book. I knew Marie Curie was a badass, but not to what extent. Now I deeply admire not only her work, but her as a person.

books.title: Pedro Páramo
books.author: Juan Rulfo
books.summary: In this classic novel we see the story of a town narrated through memories and ghosts. The story begins when Juan Preciado goes to the town to find his parent Pedro Páramo. The story deals with topics such as abuse of power, love, death, legacy and memory.
books.recommendedFor: Fans of magical realism and Mexican literature
keyTakeaways: The pursuit of power and money can corrupt and destroy entire communities
The past and the present are deeply connected by the memories and the legacy of our actions
Love and sexual desire can be very powerful forces to motivate action
Reality is a construction of subjective interpretations. Influenced by personal perspectives as well as emotions
books.personalNotes: In general it deals with important topics, but I didn't love it. The way in which it is narrated with short chapters, playing with time and characters makes it a little confusing. It is interesting to see a different Mexico, and to understand the power dynamics but I wouldn't necessarily recommend it.

books.title: The 5 Love Languages
books.author: Gary Chapman
books.summary: In this book the author talks about one of the most important topics in life, love. How we perceive and communicate love, what implications it has for relationships, and what are the main 5 languages we use. Giving practical real life examples.
books.recommendedFor: Anyone trying to improve their relationships
keyTakeaways: Forgiveness is not a feeling but a commitment to not use the offense against the offender. 'You are not a failure because you have failed.'
We are influenced by our personality, but we are not controlled by it. We can learn to communicate love in other people's language
Distinguish between love as an action and love as a feeling
1. Words of affirmation. 2. Quality time. 3. Receiving gifts. 4. Acts of service. 5. Physical touch

What does it mean to love? What is loving behavior? How do we meet each other's needs to feel loved? Is it possible to love someone that you hate? How do we balance the different stages of life with loving when people change?
books.favoriteQuote: Forgiveness is not a feeling but a commitment to not use the offense against the offender.
books.personalNotes: In general I would recommend this book to anyone trying to improve their relationships. It is insightful, enjoyable and well written. I think it is definitely a must read. Hopefully it transforms your relationships to have the love you deserve in your life. 🤍✨

books.title: Designing Your Life
books.author: Bill Burnett & Dave Evans
books.summary: A Stanford-born methodology for designing a well-lived, joyful life using design thinking principles. The authors present five core frameworks — curiosity, bias to action, reframing, awareness, and radical collaboration — to help readers prototype their way to a fulfilling life rather than searching for a single 'passion.'
books.recommendedFor: Anyone feeling stuck or wanting to approach life decisions with more creativity and intention
keyTakeaways: You don't find your passion — you develop it. Take time to cultivate interests through action, not introspection alone.
If a problem is not actionable, it is not solvable. Focus on what you can actually change.
It's OK to have three Plan A's. Life doesn't require a single path — prototype multiple possibilities.
Failure immunity comes from treating life as a continuous prototyping process. Prototypes are designed to teach you something — as you are learning, you can't fail.
Happiness is letting go of what you don't need. Correction shots are always allowed.
books.favoriteQuote: Playing the infinite game of becoming more of yourself.
books.personalNotes: This book changed the way I think about life decisions. The idea of prototyping your life rather than planning it is incredibly freeing. The failure categorization framework is practical and powerful: screwups (little stupid mistakes), weaknesses (old friends, part of your makeup), and growth opportunities (they don't have to happen again). Life is a process, not an outcome. Building your compass through a philosophy of work and a life view is something everyone should do. Highly recommend.

books.title: Hackers: Heroes of the Computer Revolution
books.author: Steven Levy
books.summary: The definitive history of the hacker movement, from the early days at MIT's Tech Model Railroad Club through the rise of personal computing. Levy profiles the digital explorers and back room geniuses who shaped the technological revolution, driven by the hacker ethic of decentralization, freedom of information, and the belief that computers can change life for the better.
books.recommendedFor: Anyone passionate about technology, programming, or the history of computing
keyTakeaways: The hacker ethic — access to computers should be unlimited, information should be free, and decentralization is key — shaped the foundation of modern tech culture.
The digital explorers who built the computer revolution were driven by curiosity and a deep belief in what technology could enable, not by money.
The tension between open access and commercialization has been present since the very beginning of computing.
True innovation often comes from the 'back room geniuses' — the people obsessed with understanding how things work at the deepest level.
books.personalNotes: A fascinating look at the origins of hacking culture and the ethic of decentralization that shaped modern technology. These were digital explorers driven by pure curiosity. Understanding where we come from in tech helps us understand where we're going.

books.title: What I Talk About When I Talk About Running
books.author: Haruki Murakami
books.summary: A memoir where Murakami reflects on the deep connection between running and writing — two disciplines that require endurance, solitude, and the willingness to push through pain. More than a book about running, it's a meditation on discipline, aging, and what it means to commit to something over a lifetime.
books.recommendedFor: Runners, writers, and anyone interested in the philosophy of discipline and endurance
keyTakeaways: Pain is inevitable, suffering is optional. The ability to endure discomfort — in running and in life — is a choice.
Running and writing share the same core requirement: the discipline to show up every day regardless of how you feel.
The solitude of long-distance running creates a unique mental space for self-reflection and creative thought.
Aging is not about decline but about adapting — finding new ways to challenge yourself as your body and mind change.
books.favoriteQuote: Pain is inevitable. Suffering is optional.
books.personalNotes: Murakami writes about running the way he writes his novels — with quiet honesty and deep introspection. The central idea that suffering is optional while pain isn't is something that applies far beyond running. A beautiful reflection on discipline and the things we choose to dedicate ourselves to.

books.title: Zeroism
books.author: Bryan Johnson
books.summary: Bryan Johnson's philosophical manifesto for being human in the age of exponential technology. The book introduces Zeroism — the intelligence of not knowing — and argues that our greatest attribute is future literacy: the ability to adapt courageously toward what we cannot yet see. It presents a framework where algorithms (Blueprint) run the human body better than we can ourselves.
books.recommendedFor: Those interested in transhumanism, longevity, and the philosophy of human-technology integration
keyTakeaways: Zeroism is the intelligence of not knowing — embracing what we do not know, what we cannot see, and acting courageously nonetheless.
Blueprint: algorithms are going to run humans because they are superior to us. A better algorithm can take care of us better than we can ourselves.
The autonomous self: we will improve ourselves to the speed of science and technology, because we can.
Future literacy — adapting toward not knowing — is our most important attribute in an age of exponential change.
Each generation builds in the context of their time. We are masters of telling fake stories to justify our behavior.
books.favoriteQuote: Each generation builds in the context of their time.
books.personalNotes: Johnson's vision is radical but thought-provoking. The idea of separating 'evening me' from 'morning me' — firing the version of yourself that makes bad decisions — is a powerful reframe. The concept of 'zero discoveries' resonates deeply: not knowing, exploring, and adapting at the pace of AI. Whether you agree with the Blueprint approach or not, the underlying philosophy of embracing uncertainty as strength rather than weakness is compelling.

books.title: Alex Karp
books.author: Alexander C. Karp & Nicholas W. Zamiska
books.summary: Palantir CEO Alex Karp argues that Silicon Valley has lost its way, prioritizing consumer apps over national security and civic duty. The book calls for a renewal of public-private collaboration in technology, particularly around AI and defense, to protect Western democratic institutions.
books.recommendedFor: Readers interested in tech leadership, data ethics, and unconventional business leaders
keyTakeaways: Silicon Valley was originally built on Pentagon-funded research serving national interests; it has since drifted toward consumerism.
The software industry must renew its commitment to addressing the AI arms race and urgent national security challenges.
A willingness to risk the disapproval of the crowd has everything to do with technological and economic outperformance.
Karp's philosophical background deeply influenced his approach — technology must serve democratic institutions, not replace them.
books.favoriteQuote: The peace and comfort that these inventions created turned Silicon Valley into a consumerist hotbed, where founders neglect their duty to help protect the American national project.
books.personalNotes: Whether you agree with Karp's politics or not, the argument is provocative and well-constructed. The idea that the tech industry has a civic responsibility beyond building consumer products is something worth debating. His philosophical roots show — this reads more like a manifesto than a memoir.

books.title: The Art of Spending Money
books.author: Morgan Housel
books.summary: From the author of The Psychology of Money, this book explores the complex relationship between spending, happiness, and identity. Housel examines how we make financial decisions, what our spending reveals about our values, and why the way we use money matters as much as how we earn or save it.
books.recommendedFor: Anyone who has read The Psychology of Money or wants to develop a healthier relationship with spending
keyTakeaways: Spending is the ultimate expression of your values — how you spend reveals who you truly are more than what you say.
The gap between what you can afford and what you spend is the most important financial metric. It's not about deprivation, it's about alignment.
Money's greatest value is giving you control over your time. Spending that trades time for stuff works against you.
Financial decisions are deeply personal — what looks irrational from the outside often makes perfect sense given someone's unique life context.
books.personalNotes: Housel continues to be one of the best writers on money and behavior. This book complements The Psychology of Money perfectly. The core insight — that spending is a mirror of your values — is simple but profound. A quick, enjoyable read that makes you think differently about every purchase.

books.title: Carlos Slim
books.author: José Martínez
books.summary: The biography of Carlos Slim, one of the richest people in the world and Mexico's most influential business figure. The book traces his journey from a modest upbringing in Mexico City to building an empire across telecommunications, construction, and finance, revealing the strategies and philosophy behind his extraordinary success.
books.recommendedFor: Business-minded readers interested in Latin American entrepreneurship and wealth building
keyTakeaways: Slim's success was built on acquiring undervalued assets during economic crises — buying when others are selling requires conviction and patience.
Diversification across industries and long-term thinking are the foundations of lasting wealth.
Understanding the specific dynamics of emerging markets — like Mexico's privatization wave — creates unique opportunities.
Slim's philosophy centers on simplicity, austerity, and reinvesting profits rather than displaying wealth.
books.personalNotes: As a Mexican, reading about Carlos Slim's journey is both inspiring and eye-opening. His ability to see opportunity where others see crisis is remarkable. The book gives you a real understanding of how business works in Mexico and Latin America, and the scale of what one person with vision and discipline can build.

books.title: Don't Die
books.author: Bryan Johnson
books.summary: Bryan Johnson's radical manifesto on longevity and the future of human health. The book makes the case that death might not be inevitable and presents his Blueprint protocol — a data-driven, algorithm-based approach to reversing aging. It challenges our acceptance of decline and argues that we can and should take radical ownership of our biology.
books.recommendedFor: Anyone interested in longevity, biohacking, or the intersection of technology and human biology
keyTakeaways: Death might not be inevitable. With the pace of scientific progress, our generation could be the first to seriously challenge aging.
The Blueprint protocol demonstrates that algorithms can manage human health better than our own decision-making.
We accept physical decline as natural when much of it is preventable with the right data and discipline.
Radical self-experimentation and transparency about health data can accelerate scientific understanding for everyone.
books.personalNotes: Whether you think Bryan Johnson is a visionary or extreme, you can't deny the book makes you rethink your relationship with your own health. The idea that we passively accept decline when science is advancing rapidly is a powerful wake-up call. Pairs well with Zeroism for the full picture of Johnson's philosophy.

books.title: El Diablo de la Botella
books.author: Robert Louis Stevenson
books.coverImage: book-covers/2025/29 EL Diablo de la botella.webp
books.summary: A classic short story about a magical bottle containing a devil that grants its owner any wish — but the owner must sell the bottle for less than they paid for it before they die, or their soul is damned. The tale explores the tension between desire, greed, and the price we pay for getting what we want.
books.recommendedFor: Fans of classic literature, moral tales, and short fiction
keyTakeaways: Every wish has a cost. The things we desire most often come with hidden prices that compound over time.
Greed creates a trap — the more you get, the harder it becomes to let go.
True love and sacrifice are the only forces strong enough to break the cycle of selfish desire.
Stevenson uses fantasy to reveal a timeless truth about human nature: we always want more, even when 'more' destroys us.
books.personalNotes: A short but powerful story. Stevenson manages to pack an entire philosophical argument about desire, greed, and love into a tight narrative. The bottle is the perfect metaphor for the things we chase in life that end up owning us. Quick read, deep message.

books.title: El Secreto
books.author: Rhonda Byrne
books.summary: The international bestseller that introduced millions to the law of attraction — the idea that our thoughts have the power to shape our reality. The book compiles teachings from various thinkers and philosophers to argue that positive thinking, visualization, and gratitude can transform every area of your life.
books.recommendedFor: Readers open to exploring the power of mindset and positive thinking
keyTakeaways: Your thoughts become things — what you focus on consistently shapes the reality you experience.
Gratitude is one of the most powerful tools for shifting your mental state and attracting positive outcomes.
Visualization is not just wishful thinking — it aligns your subconscious mind with your goals and opens you to opportunities.
The law of attraction works in both directions: negative thoughts and fear attract negative outcomes just as powerfully.
books.personalNotes: Whether you fully buy into the law of attraction or not, the core message about the power of mindset and intentional focus is valuable. At the very least, it makes you more aware of your thought patterns and how they influence your behavior and decisions.

books.title: How to Make a Few Billion Dollars
books.author: Brad Jacobs
books.coverImage: book-covers/2025/31 How to make a few billion dollars.webp
books.summary: Serial entrepreneur Brad Jacobs distills the principles behind building multiple billion-dollar companies. Drawing from his experience creating seven companies from scratch — including XPO Logistics — Jacobs shares a practical, no-nonsense framework for identifying opportunities, building teams, and scaling businesses to extraordinary size.
books.recommendedFor: Ambitious entrepreneurs and anyone interested in scaling businesses
keyTakeaways: Pattern recognition across industries is one of the most valuable entrepreneurial skills — Jacobs built billion-dollar companies in completely different sectors.
The quality of your team determines everything. Hiring exceptional people and giving them room to operate is the highest-leverage activity.
Acquisitions, when done right, can be the fastest path to scale — but discipline in valuation and integration is what separates success from disaster.
Simplicity in strategy wins. The best business plans can be explained in a few sentences.
books.personalNotes: Practical and direct — Jacobs doesn't waste time on theory. He's done it multiple times across different industries, which gives his advice a level of credibility that's rare in business books. The emphasis on team quality and pattern recognition across industries is particularly valuable.

books.title: I'm Glad My Mom Died
books.author: Jennette McCurdy
books.summary: A brutally honest memoir by the former child star of iCarly. McCurdy recounts her experience growing up under an emotionally abusive and controlling mother who pushed her into acting, and the long journey toward healing after her mother's death. It's raw, funny, and heartbreaking in equal measure.
books.recommendedFor: Anyone interested in memoirs, mental health, or understanding the hidden costs of child stardom
keyTakeaways: The title is not cruel — it's the honest recognition that sometimes a parent's death is the beginning of your freedom to become yourself.
Emotional abuse from a parent can be invisible to outsiders while being devastating to the child. Recognizing it is the first step to healing.
Recovery from eating disorders and mental health struggles is not linear — it requires honesty, professional help, and immense courage.
The entertainment industry's exploitation of child actors is a systemic problem that goes far beyond individual cases.
books.personalNotes: One of the most honest books I've read. McCurdy's ability to find humor in deeply painful experiences makes the memoir both accessible and powerful. The provocative title perfectly captures the complexity of toxic parent-child relationships. An important read about breaking free from the patterns imposed on us.

books.title: Psicópatas
books.summary: An exploration of psychopathy — what defines a psychopath, how they think, and how they operate in society. The book examines the clinical characteristics, the neuroscience behind the condition, and the disturbing reality that psychopaths are not just in prisons but in boardrooms, politics, and everyday life.
books.recommendedFor: Readers interested in criminal psychology, behavioral science, and understanding dark personality traits
keyTakeaways: Psychopathy is a spectrum — not all psychopaths are violent criminals. Many are high-functioning members of society in positions of power.
The lack of empathy and remorse combined with superficial charm makes psychopaths exceptionally skilled at manipulation.
Understanding psychopathic traits helps you recognize and protect yourself from manipulation in personal and professional relationships.
Neuroscience reveals structural differences in psychopathic brains, particularly in areas related to empathy and impulse control.
books.personalNotes: A chilling but fascinating read. Understanding how psychopaths think and operate is surprisingly practical — it helps you recognize manipulation patterns in everyday life. The most unsettling insight is how many psychopathic traits are rewarded in competitive environments.

books.title: You Only Have to Be Right Once
books.author: Randall Lane
books.summary: A Forbes collection profiling the rise of instant tech billionaires — from Snapchat's Evan Spiegel to Spotify's Daniel Ek. The book captures the stories of entrepreneurs who bet big on a single idea and won, showing that in the world of technology, you only need to be spectacularly right once to change everything.
books.recommendedFor: Aspiring entrepreneurs and anyone fascinated by how tech empires are built
keyTakeaways: In technology, one breakthrough idea executed with conviction can generate more value than decades of incremental work.
Most of these founders faced massive rejection and skepticism before their moment — persistence through doubt is the common thread.
Timing matters enormously. The same idea launched two years earlier or later might have failed entirely.
Youth and inexperience can be advantages — these founders weren't burdened by 'how things have always been done.'
books.personalNotes: Inspiring collection of stories that reinforces a key idea: you don't need to get everything right, you just need one big bet to land. The profiles are well-written and give you insight into the specific moments and decisions that made these companies possible.

books.title: Unleash the Power Within
books.author: Tony Robbins
books.summary: Tony Robbins' guide to unlocking your full potential by mastering your emotional state, beliefs, and physical energy. The book distills the principles from his legendary seminars — covering how to take control of your emotions, transform your body, enhance your relationships, and achieve your financial goals through radical personal responsibility.
books.recommendedFor: Anyone looking for a high-energy guide to personal transformation and peak performance
keyTakeaways: Your emotional state drives everything — learn to control your state and you control your outcomes.
Beliefs are the invisible architecture of your life. Limiting beliefs create limiting results.
The quality of your life is the quality of your questions. Better questions lead to better answers and better decisions.
Energy management — through nutrition, movement, and breathing — is the foundation of sustained peak performance.
books.personalNotes: High-energy and practical. Robbins has a way of making you feel like you can take on the world. The emphasis on state management — the idea that controlling your emotional and physical state is the master skill — is something I keep coming back to. Whether or not you love his style, the frameworks are genuinely useful.
